title: Robust Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Small UAS Separation Assurance under GPS Degradation and Spoofing
abstract: We address robust separation assurance for small Unmanned Aircraft Systems (sUAS) under GPS degradation and spoofing via Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL). In cooperative surveillance, each aircraft (or agent) broadcasts its GPS-derived position; when such position broadcasts are corrupted, the entire observed air traffic state becomes unreliable. We cast this state observation corruption as a zero-sum game between the agents and an adversary: with probability R, the adversary perturbs the observed state to maximally degrade each agent's safety performance. We derive a closed-form expression for this adversarial perturbation, bypassing adversarial training entirely and enabling linear-time evaluation in the state dimension. We show that this expression approximates the true worst-case adversarial perturbation with second-order accuracy. We further bound the safety performance gap between clean and corrupted observations, showing that it degrades at most linearly with the corruption probability under Kullback-Leibler regularization. Finally, we integrate the closed-form adversarial policy into a MARL policy gradient algorithm to obtain a robust counter-policy for the agents. In a high-density sUAS simulation, we observe near-zero collision rates under corruption levels up to 35%, outperforming a baseline policy trained without adversarial perturbations.

sections.heading: IIntroduction
sections.text:
The proliferation of small Unmanned Aircraft Systems (sUAS) in urban airspaces is reshaping logistics and emergency response. Commercial operators, including Zipline and Wing, now conduct beyond-visual-line-of-sight package deliveries under FAA authorization [ 8 , 16 ] . As these operations scale, autonomous separation assurance becomes imperative: vehicles must avoid conflicts without human intervention, relying entirely on onboard sensing and cooperative information exchange.

This autonomy, however, rests on fragile infrastructure. Small UAS depend on Global Positioning System (GPS) for position and velocity estimation, yet urban canyons induce multipath errors of 10–60 m [ 18 , 11 ] , and GPS signals are vulnerable to spoofing attacks with field-demonstrated success rates of 5–40% [ 22 , 15 ] .
Moreover, unlike commercial aviation’s radar-verified surveillance, sUAS traffic management relies on cooperative position sharing via Remote Identification (Remote ID) [ 7 ] , where each broadcast is itself derived from the vehicle’s onboard GPS receiver. When GPS is corrupted, an sUAS’s observation includes not only its own erroneous position but also the corrupted broadcasts of every vehicle in its vicinity. The resulting joint state observation may thus be adversarially perturbed in every component simultaneously, with perturbations correlated through a shared corruption source.

sections.heading: Related Work and Contributions
sections.text:
Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) has emerged as a compelling paradigm for separation assurance. Brittain et al. [ 2 ] demonstrated effective conflict resolution using attention-based architectures that scale gracefully to variable intruder counts, while Chen et al. [ 3 ] integrated tactical deconfliction with strategic traffic demand management. These approaches achieve impressive performance but assume perfect state observation, an assumption incompatible with GPS-dependent cooperative surveillance.

The vulnerability of learned policies to observation perturbations has motivated robust training methods. Adversarial approaches expose agents to gradient-based attacks [ 10 , 13 ] or learned adversaries [ 19 , 9 ] . The Robust Markov Decision Process (MDP) framework captures worst-case outcomes via uncertainty sets over transitions [ 14 , 17 ] . Recent extensions address observation-level attacks: Zhang et al. [ 25 ] introduced state-adversarial perturbations for single-agent settings, while Wang and Zou [ 24 ] developed policy gradients under R R -contamination, where the state transition itself is corrupted with probability R R . These state-adversarial formulations are well-suited to GPS spoofing, where vehicle dynamics are nominal, but observation is degraded. However, existing work addresses single-agent settings and does not consider the correlated full-state corruption arising in cooperative multi-agent surveillance.

Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) offer an alternative path to safety via forward-invariance guarantees [ 1 ] . Neural CBF extensions [ 5 ] and multi-agent formulations such as GCBF++ [ 21 ] have shown promise. Yet, CBF methods often require known dynamics, access to true state observations, or typically centralized coordination, all of which are assumptions violated precisely when GPS is unreliable and agents must act on local, potentially corrupted information received through a cooperative surveillance network.

To the best of our knowledge, no existing framework addresses robust multi-agent separation assurance under full-state observation corruption, where both the controlled aircraft’s and neighboring aircraft’s positions may be simultaneously degraded, while providing formal performance guarantees and supporting decentralized execution. We bridge this gap by combining R R -contamination modeling of the complete traffic picture with analytical adversarial optimization and decision-invariance regularization. To that end, our contributions are as follows.

1. We cast GPS observation corruption as a zero-sum game between the adversarial and the UAS agents, and derive a closed-form approximate solution for the worst-case adversarial perturbation (Theorem 1 ). 2. We prove that this closed-form adversarial perturbation approximates the true worst-case perturbation with error that is second-order in the perturbation magnitude (Theorem 2 ). 3. We prove that Kullback-Leibler (KL)-based policy regularization between clean and adversarial observations bounds the expected performance loss induced by corrupted observations, yielding a principled robustness-performance tradeoff (Proposition 1 ). 4. In high-density sUAS simulation, the robust policy achieves near-zero collision rates under corruption levels up to 35 % 35\% , outperforming a baseline policy trained without adversarial perturbations.

Notation: Bold symbols denote matrices. We write ⟨ 𝑨 , 𝑩 ⟩ = ∑ i ​ j A i ​ j ​ B i ​ j \langle\bm{A},\bm{B}\rangle=\sum_{ij}A_{ij}B_{ij} for the Frobenius inner product, 𝑨 ⊙ 𝑩 \bm{A}\odot\bm{B} for element-wise multiplication, and sign ​ ( 𝑨 ) \mathrm{sign}(\bm{A}) for the element-wise sign operator. ∥ ⋅ ∥ p \|\cdot\|_{p} is the L p L_{p} -norm operator. | ⋅ | TV = 1 2 ∥ ⋅ ∥ 1 |\cdot|_{\mathrm{TV}}=\frac{1}{2}\|\cdot\|_{1} denotes the total variation distance operator. Inequalities between matrices are understood component-wise . Absolute values are taken element-wise. For a set 𝒳 \mathcal{X} , Δ ​ ( 𝒳 ) \Delta(\mathcal{X}) denotes the ( | 𝒳 | − 1 |\mathcal{X}|-1 )-dimensional probability simplex on 𝒳 \mathcal{X} . For a variable x ∈ ℝ x\in\mathbb{R} and fixed values a , b a,b , clip ​ ( x , a , b ) \mathrm{clip}(x,a,b) clamps x x to [ a , b ] [a,b] .

sections.heading: IIProblem Formulation
sections.text: We study autonomous separation assurance for sUAS operating in a structured low-altitude airspace, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . Each aircraft follows a predefined route through a sequence of waypoints. The control objective for an sUAS agent is to regulate its speed so as to maintain safe separation from nearby traffic while preserving efficient progress toward its destination. We formulate this problem as decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning: N N homogeneous agents (with identical dynamics) apply the same learned policy using local observations, with each agent viewing itself as the ownship and nearby aircraft as intruders .

sections.heading: II-AStates, Actions, and Dynamics
sections.text:
At time t t , the true local traffic state from the ownship’s viewpoint is denoted by 𝑺 t ∈ 𝒮 \bm{S}_{t}\in\mathcal{S} , which collects information relevant for separation assurance, including the ownship’s progress and speed along its route, together with the relative geometric and kinematic information of nearby intruders.

For each ownship, the state is represented as 𝑺 t ∈ ℝ ( 1 + m ) × n \bm{S}_{t}\in\mathbb{R}^{(1+m)\times n} , where the first row corresponds to the ownship and the remaining rows correspond to up to m m nearby intruders. Since all rows of 𝑺 t \bm{S}_{t} are derived from GPS (both the ownship’s own coordinates and the intruders’ broadcasts), GPS corruption can affect the entire state matrix simultaneously, coupling observation errors across all ( 1 + m ) (1+m) rows.

Each aircraft state is given by s = [ x , y , ψ , v , d g , u − ] ∈ ℝ 1 × n s=[x,~y,~\psi,~v,~d_{g},~u_{-}]\in\mathbb{R}^{1\times n} with n = 6 n=6 , where ( x , y ) (x,y) denotes planar position, ψ \psi heading, v v speed, d g d_{g} distance-to-destination, and u − u_{-} the previous action. The action space consists of discrete speed adjustments 𝒜 = { − δ ​ v , 0 , + δ ​ v } \mathcal{A}=\{-\delta v,0,+\delta v\} , where δ ​ v = 5 \delta v=5 knots [ 2 , 6 ] . The action u t ∈ 𝒜 u_{t}\in\mathcal{A} is a guidance-level speed command tracked by the vehicle’s inner-loop flight controller.

The aircraft state transitions are modeled by discrete-time point-mass kinematics [ 12 ] s t + 1 = f ​ ( s t , u t ) = a ​ ( s t ) + b ​ ( s t ) ​ u t s_{t+1}=f(s_{t},u_{t})=a(s_{t})+b(s_{t})u_{t} (1) with a ​ ( s ) = [ x + ( v ​ cos ⁡ ψ + w E ) ​ Δ ​ t y + ( v ​ sin ⁡ ψ + w N ) ​ Δ ​ t h ​ ( ψ ) v d g − v ​ Δ ​ t 0 ] , b ​ ( s ) = [ cos ⁡ ψ ​ Δ ​ t sin ⁡ ψ ​ Δ ​ t 0 1 − Δ ​ t 1 ] . a(s)=\begin{bmatrix}x{+}(v\cos\psi{+}w_{E})\Delta t\\
y{+}(v\sin\psi{+}w_{N})\Delta t\\
h(\psi)\\
v\\
d_{g}-v\Delta t\\
0\end{bmatrix},~b(s)=\begin{bmatrix}\cos\psi\,\Delta t\\
\sin\psi\,\Delta t\\
0\\
1\\
-\Delta t\\
1\end{bmatrix}. (2) The terms ( w E , w N ) (w_{E},w_{N}) are wind components, Δ ​ t = 1 ​ s \Delta t=1\,\text{s} is the integration time step, and h ​ ( ψ ) h(\psi) updates the route heading at waypoint transitions. This guidance-level model captures the traffic evolution relevant to separation assurance as used in traffic-level simulations [ 2 , 12 ] .

sections.heading: II-BMarkov Decision Process with Adversarial Observation Corruption
sections.text:
We model the robust sUAS separation and navigation problem as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) with adversarially corrupted observations. The MDP tuple ( 𝒮 , 𝒜 , P , r , γ ) (\mathcal{S},\mathcal{A},P,r,\gamma) is described by a state space 𝒮 \mathcal{S} , an action space 𝒜 \mathcal{A} , a transition kernel P = { p 𝑺 u ∈ Δ ​ ( 𝒮 ) , u ∈ 𝒜 , 𝑺 ∈ 𝒮 } P=\{p^{u}_{\bm{S}}\in\Delta(\mathcal{S}),u\in\mathcal{A},\bm{S}\in\mathcal{S}\} , where p 𝑺 u p^{u}_{\bm{S}} is the distribution of the next state over 𝒮 \mathcal{S} upon taking action u u in state 𝑺 \bm{S} ; a reward function r : 𝒮 × 𝒜 → ℝ r:\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{A}\to\mathbb{R} ; and a discount factor γ ∈ [ 0 , 1 ) \gamma\in[0,1) .

Let 𝑺 ~ t \tilde{\bm{S}}_{t} denote the observation available to the agent at time t t . This observation may be corrupted by spoofing or sensor errors, so it may be different from the true state 𝑺 t {\bm{S}}_{t} . However, since the observation and the true state have the same structure, we write the agent’s policy as π : 𝒮 → Δ ​ ( 𝒜 ) \pi:\mathcal{S}\to\Delta(\mathcal{A}) with action sampled according to u t ∼ π ( ⋅ ∣ 𝑺 ~ t ) u_{t}\sim\pi(\cdot\mid\tilde{\bm{S}}_{t}) .
After the agent applies action u t u_{t} , the environment evolves to the next true state 𝑺 t + 1 ∼ p 𝑺 t u t \bm{S}_{t+1}\sim p_{\bm{S}_{t}}^{u_{t}} and produces reward r ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 , u t ) r(\bm{S}_{t+1},u_{t}) .

In contrast to classical robust MDP formulations [ 14 , 17 ] which place uncertainty on the transition kernel, we consider uncertainty in the agent’s observation. This choice is motivated by GPS spoofing and related sensor attacks: the physical airspace state evolves according to nominal dynamics, but the agent may act on a corrupted observation of that state. Such state-adversarial models have been studied in robust reinforcement learning [ 25 , 24 ] and are well aligned with empirical evidence on Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) spoofing in UAV systems [ 22 , 15 , 20 ] .

We model GPS corruption through R R -contamination uncertainty. Specifically, conditional on the true next state 𝑺 t + 1 \bm{S}_{t+1} , the observation 𝑺 ~ t + 1 \tilde{\bm{S}}_{t+1} available to the agent is generated as 𝑺 ~ t + 1 ∼ O R ( ⋅ ∣ 𝑺 t + 1 ) , \tilde{\bm{S}}_{t+1}\sim O_{R}(\cdot\mid\bm{S}_{t+1}), (3) where R ∈ [ 0 , 1 ) R\in[0,1) denotes the corruption probability.

Thus, with probability 1 − R 1-R the next observation is accurate, and with probability R R it is replaced by an adversarial observation within the bounded uncertainty set. The uncertainty bound 𝜿 \bm{\kappa} reflects physically plausible GPS and sensing errors. In particular, the position components are calibrated to empirically reported urban GNSS error magnitudes [ 18 ] . This corruption model captures bounded but potentially adversarial deviations in the agent’s perceived traffic configuration.

A key modeling choice is that the reward depends on the true next state 𝑺 t + 1 \bm{S}_{t+1} rather than the corrupted observation 𝑺 ~ t + 1 \tilde{\bm{S}}_{t+1} . This reflects the fact that collisions and separation violations are physical events: they occur in the real airspace regardless of what the agent perceives. Since training is performed offline in simulation, computing such true rewards is realistic.

sections.heading: II-CReward
sections.text:
The reward is computed from the true next state and encourages safe separation with minimal speed changes. Specifically, we design it as

r ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 , u t ) = ∑ j = 1 m q ​ ( d o ​ j ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) ) + g ​ ( u t ) , r(\bm{S}_{t+1},u_{t})=\sum_{j=1}^{m}q\!\left(d_{oj}(\bm{S}_{t+1})\right)+g(u_{t}), (6) where d o ​ j ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) = ‖ ( x t + 1 o , y t + 1 o ) − ( x t + 1 j , y t + 1 j ) ‖ 2 d_{oj}(\bm{S}_{t+1})=\left\|(x^{o}_{t+1},y^{o}_{t+1})-(x^{j}_{t+1},y^{j}_{t+1})\right\|_{2} (7) is the distance between the ownship o o and intruder j j . The function q ​ ( ⋅ ) q(\cdot) penalizes unsafe proximity, i.e. loss-of-separation, while the function g ​ ( ⋅ ) g(\cdot) penalizes control effort.

sections.heading: II-DRobust Objective
sections.text:
Given this reward, the value function of a stationary policy π \pi (evaluated on the true state) is: V π ( 𝑺 t ) = 𝔼 π [ ∑ k = 0 ∞ γ k r ( 𝑺 t + k + 1 , u t + k ) | 𝑺 t ] . V^{\pi}(\bm{S}_{t})=\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{k}r(\bm{S}_{t+k+1},u_{t+k})\,\middle|\,\bm{S}_{t}\right]. (8) However, under observation corruption, the policy no longer acts on the true state 𝑺 t \bm{S}_{t} but on the corrupted observation 𝑺 ~ t \tilde{\bm{S}}_{t} . The controlled agent’s performance is therefore affected through the selected action and, recursively, through the future value of the resulting trajectory.

Conditioned on the true next state 𝑺 t + 1 \bm{S}_{t+1} , the R R -contamination model implies that the next observation is clean with probability 1 − R 1-R and adversarial with probability R R . Accordingly, for a given action u t u_{t} , we define the one-step robust value objective as 𝒥 rob π ​ ( 𝑺 t , u t , 𝚵 t + 1 ) \displaystyle\mathcal{J}^{\pi}_{\mathrm{rob}}(\bm{S}_{t},u_{t},\bm{\Xi}_{t+1}) = r ( 𝑺 t + 1 , u t ) + γ [ ( 1 − R ) V π ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) \displaystyle=r(\bm{S}_{t+1},u_{t})+\gamma\Bigl[(1-R)V^{\pi}(\bm{S}_{t+1}) + R V π ( 𝚵 t + 1 ) ] , \displaystyle+R\,V^{\pi}(\bm{\Xi}_{t+1})\Bigr], (9) where 𝑺 t + 1 ∼ p 𝑺 t u t \bm{S}_{t+1}\sim p_{\bm{S}_{t}}^{u_{t}} is the true next state and 𝚵 t + 1 ∈ Ω ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) \bm{\Xi}_{t+1}\in\Omega(\bm{S}_{t+1}) is chosen within the uncertainty set ( 5 ).
The worst-case adversarial observation is then defined by 𝚵 t + 1 ⋆ ∈ arg ⁡ min 𝚵 ∈ Ω ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) ​ 𝒥 rob π ​ ( 𝑺 t , u t , 𝚵 ) . \bm{\Xi}_{t+1}^{\star}\in\underset{\bm{\Xi}\in\Omega(\bm{S}_{t+1})}{\arg\min}\mathcal{J}^{\pi}_{\mathrm{rob}}(\bm{S}_{t},u_{t},\bm{\Xi}). (10) Since r ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 , u t ) r(\bm{S}_{t+1},u_{t}) and V π ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) V^{\pi}(\bm{S}_{t+1}) do not depend on 𝚵 \bm{\Xi} , the minimization is equivalently 𝚵 t + 1 ⋆ ∈ arg ⁡ min 𝚵 ∈ Ω ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) ​ V π ​ ( 𝚵 ) . \bm{\Xi}_{t+1}^{\star}\in\underset{\bm{\Xi}\in\Omega(\bm{S}_{t+1})}{\arg\min}\,V^{\pi}(\bm{\Xi}). (11) This yields the one-step robust value target of policy π \pi V rob π ​ ( 𝑺 t ) \displaystyle V^{\pi}_{\mathrm{rob}}(\bm{S}_{t}) = 𝔼 π [ r ( 𝑺 t + 1 , u t ) + γ ( ( 1 − R ) V π ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) \displaystyle=\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\Bigl[r(\bm{S}_{t+1},u_{t})+\gamma\bigl((1-R)V^{\pi}(\bm{S}_{t+1}) + R min 𝚵 ∈ Ω ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) V π ( 𝚵 ) ) | 𝑺 t ] . \displaystyle+R\min_{\bm{\Xi}\in\Omega(\bm{S}_{t+1})}V^{\pi}(\bm{\Xi})\bigr)\,\Big|\,\bm{S}_{t}\Bigr]. (12) The corresponding robust control objective for the aircraft is to find a policy that maximizes expected discounted return under this worst-case bounded corruption model: π ⋆ ∈ arg ⁡ max π ⁡ 𝔼 ​ [ V rob π ​ ( 𝑺 0 ) ] . \pi^{\star}\in\arg\max_{\pi}~\mathbb{E}\!\left[V^{\pi}_{\mathrm{rob}}(\bm{S}_{0})\right]. (13) However, direct optimization of ( 10 )–( 13 ) is generally intractable for nonlinear value approximators. We therefore develop a tractable policy-gradient method based on a first-order characterization of the worst-case adversarial observation, coupled with regularization that promotes perturbation-invariant policy behavior.

sections.heading: III-AFirst-Order Worst-Case Adversarial Observation
sections.text:
The robust objective in Section II-D requires solving the inner minimization in ( 13 ) described by ( 12 ) and ( 11 ).
To that end, we make the following assumption.

This assumption is standard for local first-order analysis and is reasonable for smooth neural value approximators on bounded neighborhoods of the state space.
Under such assumption, a first-order Taylor expansion of V π ​ ( 𝚵 ) V^{\pi}(\bm{\Xi}) around 𝑺 t + 1 \bm{S}_{t+1} yields V π ​ ( 𝚵 ) = V π ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) + ⟨ ∇ V π ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) , 𝚵 − 𝑺 t + 1 ⟩ + o ​ ( ‖ 𝚵 − 𝑺 t + 1 ‖ ) . V^{\pi}(\bm{\Xi})=V^{\pi}(\bm{S}_{t+1})+\left\langle\nabla V^{\pi}(\bm{S}_{t+1}),\,\bm{\Xi}-\bm{S}_{t+1}\right\rangle\\
+o(\|\bm{\Xi}-\bm{S}_{t+1}\|). (14) Neglecting higher-order terms inside o ​ ( ⋅ ) o(\cdot) , we approximate min 𝚵 ∈ Ω ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) V π ( 𝚵 ) ≈ min 𝚵 ∈ Ω ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) [ V π ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) + ⟨ ∇ V π ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) , 𝚵 − 𝑺 t + 1 ⟩ ] . \min_{\bm{\Xi}\in\Omega(\bm{S}_{t+1})}V^{\pi}(\bm{\Xi})\;\approx\;\min_{\bm{\Xi}\in\Omega(\bm{S}_{t+1})}\bigl[V^{\pi}(\bm{S}_{t+1})\\
+\bigl\langle\nabla V^{\pi}(\bm{S}_{t+1}),\,\bm{\Xi}-\bm{S}_{t+1}\bigr\rangle\bigr]. (15) This leads to a closed-form expression for the worst-case adversarial perturbation, given in the following theorem.

Using Theorem 1 , we also immediately get a closed-form expression for the worst-case value function as follows.

Next, we also quantify the accuracy of this first-order approximation, and we further assume local smoothness of the value function to that end.

Theorem 2 provides a local approximation guarantee for the first-order adversarial surrogate: the gap between the exact worst-case value and its closed-form estimate is second-order in the corruption radius. Hence, for sufficiently small bounded observation perturbations, the first-order adversarial observation captures the dominant degradation in the value function of next state.

sections.heading: III-BRobust PPO with Shared Actor-Critic
sections.text:
Having established a closed-form expression for the worst-case adversarial perturbation, i.e., for the minimizer in ( 12 ),
we solve ( 13 ) using proximal policy optimization (PPO) [ 23 ] with a shared actor-critic network. Training is centralized in the sense that trajectories collected by all agents are aggregated to update a single set of shared parameters, whereas execution is decentralized: each sUAS applies the learned policy using only its own local observation.

We parameterize the policy with an actor π θ \pi_{\theta} and the value function with a critic V ϕ V_{\phi} . As shown in Fig. 2 , the actor and critic share a common observation encoder and differ only in their output heads.

Training proceeds offline and has two phases. In the first phase, we pretrain the architecture under clean observations ( R = 0 R=0 ) using standard PPO, yielding a set of frozen parameters θ ¯ , ϕ ¯ \bar{\theta},\bar{\phi} . In the second phase, we initialize another set of trainable parameters θ , ϕ \theta,\phi and optimize them under R R -contaminated observations: at each transition, the true next state 𝑺 t + 1 \bm{S}_{t+1} is observed with probability 1 − R 1-R , and replaced by the adversarial perturbation (derived in Subsection III-A ) 𝚵 t + 1 ⋆ , FO = 𝑺 t + 1 − 𝜿 ⊙ sign ​ ( ∇ V ϕ ¯ ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) ) \bm{\Xi}_{t+1}^{\star,\mathrm{FO}}=\bm{S}_{t+1}-\bm{\kappa}\odot\mathrm{sign}\bigl(\nabla V_{\bar{\phi}}(\bm{S}_{t+1})\bigr) (25) with probability R R . The frozen V ϕ ¯ V_{\bar{\phi}} ensures that the adversary targets consistent vulnerabilities rather than co-adapting with the policy.

The actor and critic losses follow standard PPO, computed on contaminated trajectories. Specifically, let R ^ t R \hat{R}_{t}^{R} denote the return target computed from the sampled trajectory under ( 4 ), and let A ^ t R \hat{A}_{t}^{R} denote the corresponding advantage estimate. The value head is trained by minimizing the squared error between its prediction and the realized return impacted by the R R -contamination: ℒ V ​ ( ϕ ) = 𝔼 t ​ [ ( V ϕ ​ ( 𝑺 ~ t ) − R ^ t R ) 2 ] . \mathcal{L}_{V}(\phi)=\mathbb{E}_{t}\Bigl[\bigl(V_{\phi}(\tilde{\bm{S}}_{t})-\hat{R}_{t}^{R}\bigr)^{2}\Bigr]. (26) The actor is updated by the PPO clipped surrogate ℒ clip ​ ( θ ) = − 𝔼 t ​ [ min ⁡ ( ρ t ​ ( θ ) ​ A ^ t R , clip ​ ( ρ t ​ ( θ ) , 1 − ϵ , 1 + ϵ ) ​ A ^ t R ) ] , \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{clip}}(\theta)=-\mathbb{E}_{t}\bigg[\min\Bigl(\rho_{t}(\theta)\hat{A}_{t}^{R},\,\mathrm{clip}\bigl(\rho_{t}(\theta),1-\epsilon,1+\epsilon\bigr)\hat{A}_{t}^{R}\Bigr)\bigg], (27) where ρ t ​ ( θ ) = π θ ​ ( u t ∣ 𝑺 ~ t ) π θ old ​ ( u t ∣ 𝑺 ~ t ) . \rho_{t}(\theta)=\frac{\pi_{\theta}(u_{t}\mid\tilde{\bm{S}}_{t})}{\pi_{\theta_{\mathrm{old}}}(u_{t}\mid\tilde{\bm{S}}_{t})}. (28) Here θ old \theta_{\mathrm{old}} denotes the policy parameters before the update during training and ϵ = 0.2 \epsilon=0.2 is a hyparameter [ 23 ] . An entropy bonus is added in the usual way to encourage exploration [ 23 ] .

Note that the training structure follows standard PPO, with observations probabilistically replaced by the closed-form adversarial perturbation ( 25 ). The regularization terms introduced next further encourage the policy to behave consistently under clean and corrupted observations while preserving nominal safe separation performance.

sections.heading: III-CPerturbation-Invariant Policy Regularization
sections.text:
We augment the robust PPO objective with two complementary Kullback-Leibler
(KL) regularizers: an invariance term that aligns the current policy on clean and adversarial observations, and an anchoring term that keeps the learned policy close to the pretrained nominal policy on clean observations.

Let π θ ¯ \pi_{\bar{\theta}} denote the pretrained nominal teacher policy. For the first-order adversarial observation 𝚵 t ⋆ , FO \bm{\Xi}_{t}^{\star,\mathrm{FO}} defined in ( 25 ), we define the perturbation-invariance regularizer as ℒ inv ( θ ) = 𝔼 t [ D KL ( π θ ( ⋅ ∣ 𝑺 t ) ∥ π θ ( ⋅ ∣ 𝚵 t ⋆ , FO ) ) ] . \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{inv}}(\theta)=\mathbb{E}_{t}\bigg[D_{\mathrm{KL}}\bigl(\pi_{\theta}(\cdot\mid\bm{S}_{t})~\big\|~\pi_{\theta}(\cdot\mid\bm{\Xi}_{t}^{\star,\mathrm{FO}})\bigr)\bigg]. (29) This term penalizes the policy sensitivity to observation corruption, encouraging similar decisions under clean and perturbed inputs. Moreover, the teacher-anchor regularizer is ℒ anchor ( θ ) = 𝔼 t [ D KL ( π θ ¯ ( ⋅ ∣ 𝑺 t ) ∥ π θ ( ⋅ ∣ 𝑺 t ) ) ] . \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{anchor}}(\theta)=\mathbb{E}_{t}\bigg[D_{\mathrm{KL}}\bigl(\pi_{\bar{\theta}}(\cdot\mid\bm{S}_{t})~\big\|~\pi_{\theta}(\cdot\mid\bm{S}_{t})\bigr)\bigg]. (30) This term discourages unnecessary drift from the nominal teacher on clean observations, ensuring that robustness does not come at the cost of performance. Thus, the complete actor objective becomes ℒ actor ​ ( θ ) = ℒ clip ​ ( θ ) + λ inv ​ ℒ inv ​ ( θ ) + λ anchor ​ ℒ anchor ​ ( θ ) , \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{actor}}(\theta)=\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{clip}}(\theta)+\lambda_{\mathrm{inv}}\,\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{inv}}(\theta)+\lambda_{\mathrm{anchor}}\,\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{anchor}}(\theta), (31) where λ inv , λ anchor ≥ 0 \lambda_{\mathrm{inv}},\lambda_{\mathrm{anchor}}\geq 0 are regularization weights.

The following proposition shows that controlling the invariance regularizer (so that 𝔼 𝑺 [ D KL ( π θ ( ⋅ ∣ 𝑺 ) ∥ π θ ( ⋅ ∣ 𝚵 ⋆ ) ) ] ≤ B \mathbb{E}_{\bm{S}}\!\left[D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\left(\pi_{\theta}(\cdot\mid\bm{S})~\middle\|~\pi_{\theta}(\cdot\mid\bm{\Xi}^{\star})\right)\right]\leq B )
directly bounds the one-step decision degradation induced by acting on corrupted observations. To that end, we define the state-action value function for a policy π \pi : Q π ​ ( 𝑺 t , u t ) = 𝔼 ​ [ r ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 , u t ) + γ ​ V π ​ ( 𝑺 t + 1 ) ∣ 𝑺 t , u t ] . Q^{\pi}(\bm{S}_{t},u_{t})=\mathbb{E}[r(\bm{S}_{t+1},u_{t})+\gamma V^{\pi}(\bm{S}_{t+1})\mid\bm{S}_{t},u_{t}].

Proposition 1 bounds the one-step degradation or spoofing attack, and the next corollary generalizes the bound over the full planning horizon.

In practice, the regularization weight λ inv \lambda_{\mathrm{inv}} in ( 31 ) controls the achieved invariance budget B B : larger λ inv \lambda_{\mathrm{inv}} drives ℒ inv \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{inv}} toward zero, reducing B B and tightening the bounds in ( 32 ) and ( 35 ).

sections.heading: III-DOverall Training Objective and Procedure
sections.text:
The overall training objective combines the losses from the previous subsections: ℒ total ​ ( θ , ϕ ) = ℒ clip ​ ( θ ) + c V ​ ℒ V ​ ( ϕ ) − c H ​ ℋ ​ ( π θ ) \displaystyle\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{total}}(\theta,\phi)=\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{clip}}(\theta)+c_{V}\,\mathcal{L}_{V}(\phi)-c_{H}\,\mathcal{H}(\pi_{\theta}) + λ inv ​ ℒ inv ​ ( θ ) + λ anchor ​ ℒ anchor ​ ( θ ) , \displaystyle+\lambda_{\mathrm{inv}}\,\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{inv}}(\theta)+\lambda_{\mathrm{anchor}}\,\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{anchor}}(\theta), (36) where ℒ clip \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{clip}} is the PPO clipped surrogate in ( 27 ), ℒ V \mathcal{L}_{V} is the robust value loss in ( 26 ), ℋ ​ ( π θ ) \mathcal{H}(\pi_{\theta}) is the entropy bonus standard in PPO to encourage exploration [ 23 ] , and ℒ inv \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{inv}} and ℒ anchor \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{anchor}} are defined in ( 29 )–( 30 ). The coefficients c V , c H , λ inv , λ anchor ≥ 0 c_{V},c_{H},\lambda_{\mathrm{inv}},\lambda_{\mathrm{anchor}}\geq 0 balance value fitting, exploration, perturbation invariance, and teacher anchoring.

At each iteration, trajectories from all agents are aggregated into a centralized batch, from which robust bootstrap targets, advantages, and regularization terms are computed. The shared actor-critic parameters are then updated by minimizing ( 36 ). The complete training procedure is summarized in Algorithm 1 .

sections.heading: IVExperimental Evaluation
sections.text: We evaluate the proposed robust MARL framework in the structured en-route airspace of Fig. 1 , designed within the BlueSky air traffic simulator [ 12 ] . Traffic arrivals on each route follow a Poisson process with a minimum headway of 30 30 s. Each vehicle is modeled after the Amazon MK30 sUAS, with nominal cruise speed 20 20 m/s and admissible speed range [ 7.5 , 36 ] [7.5,36] m/s.

sections.heading: IV-ATraining Setup
sections.text:
Both nominal and robust policies use the shared actor-critic architecture shown in Fig. 2 . Training is performed over 2 × 10 6 2\times 10^{6} steps. The nominal teacher policy is first trained under clean observations ( R = 0 R=0 ) using standard PPO. The robust policy is then trained from pooled multi-agent experience using the proposed method, with a curriculum on R ∈ { 0 , 0.05 , 0.15 , 0.25 , 0.35 , 0.5 } . R\in\{0,~0.05,~0.15,~0.25,~0.35,~0.5\}. PPO hyperparameters follow standard settings, with additional regularization weights λ inv = 0.01 \lambda_{\mathrm{inv}}=0.01 and λ anchor = 0.01 \lambda_{\mathrm{anchor}}=0.01 . The learning rate is set to 2.5 × 10 − 4 2.5\times 10^{-4} and the number of epoch 8 8 . The maximum number of intruders for each agent is m = 5 m=5 . The entropy and value loss coefficient are respectively c H = 10 − 1 c_{H}=10^{-1} and c V = 0.5 c_{V}=0.5 . The protected radius or near mid-air collision (NMAC) distance threshold is set to d p ​ z = 100 d_{pz}=100 m, and for each sUAS, the intruder aircraft are detected within radius d r = 500 d_{r}=500 m. Hence the function q ​ ( ⋅ ) q(\cdot) and g ​ ( ⋅ ) g(\cdot) defined in the reward function ( 6 ) are initialized as g ​ ( x ) \displaystyle g(x) = { − α + β ​ x if ​ x ≤ d r (loss of separation) − α + β ​ x − c NMAC if ​ x ≤ d p ​ z (NMAC) , \displaystyle=\begin{cases}-\alpha+\beta x\quad\mathrm{if}\ x\leq d_{r}\quad\text{(loss of separation)}\\
-\alpha+\beta x-c_{\mathrm{NMAC}}\quad\mathrm{if}\ x\leq d_{pz}\quad\text{(NMAC)}\end{cases}, q ​ ( u ) \displaystyle q(u) = − λ u ​ ( u / δ ​ v ) 2 , \displaystyle=-\lambda_{u}(u/\delta v)^{2}, where α = 0.1 , β = 2 × 10 − 4 , c NMAC = 1 , λ u = 0.001 \alpha=0.1,~\beta=2\times 10^{-4},~c_{\mathrm{NMAC}}=1,~\lambda_{u}=0.001 are empirically chosen and δ ​ v = 5 \delta v=5 knots (Section II-A ).

The corruption bound 𝜿 \bm{\kappa} is chosen from reported urban GNSS error magnitudes (e.g., κ x = κ y = 60 ​ m , κ v = 2 ​ m / s , κ ψ = 5 ∘ \kappa_{x}=\kappa_{y}=60\mathrm{m},\kappa_{v}=2\mathrm{m/s},\kappa_{\psi}=5^{\circ} ), consistent with the uncertainty model introduced in Section II-B .

sections.heading: IV-BEvaluation Protocol
sections.text:
We compare the nominal policy trained without adversarial corruption against the proposed robust policy. Both policies are evaluated under the same conditions over a range of corruption rates R ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] R\in[0,1] . The results are averaged over 100 100 simulated episodes.

We report the following safety metrics: the number of near mid-air collisions (NMACs) and the minimum separation distance achieved during each episode on average. Lower NMAC counts and larger minimum LOS distances indicate better robustness.

sections.heading: IV-CResults and Discussion
sections.text:
Fig. 3 compares the robust and nominal policies across adversarial observation perturbation rates R ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] R\in[0,1] . Under clean observation ( R = 0 R=0 ), both policies achieve comparable safety performance, confirming that robust training does not sacrifice nominal capability. As R R increases, however, their behaviors diverge sharply.

The nominal policy maintains near-zero NMACs only through R ≈ 0.25 R\approx 0.25 after which collision counts rise steeply by reaching approximately 18 18 NMACs per episode at R = 0.95 R=0.95 . In contrast, the robust policy sustains near-zero NMACs through R ≈ 0.35 R\approx 0.35 and degrades more gracefully beyond, with roughly 5 5 NMACs at R = 0.95 R=0.95 . The minimum separation distance (right panel) tells a consistent story: the robust policy preserves larger safety margins across all corruption levels, with the gap widening as R R increases. Fig. 4 illustrates a representative traffic scenario under the robust policy at R = 0.35 R=0.35 . Despite adversarial perturbations, all aircraft maintain safe separation as they traverse the structured airspace.

Ablation Study Fig. 5 isolates the contributions of each regularization term. Two findings emerge. First, the invariance regularization without anchoring (blue) destabilizes training: NMACs are high even at R = 0 R=0 , indicating that enforcing output consistency without a stable reference collapses the learned representation. Second, anchoring alone (green) preserves nominal competence at low corruption but degrades sharply beyond R ≈ 0.5 R\approx 0.5 .
The full method (purple) achieves the strongest performance while providing the theoretical guarantees of Proposition 1 .
Together, these results validate the method’s design: the closed-form adversary (Theorem 1 ) and the complementary regularizers ( 29 )-( 30 ) drive robustness, stabilize training and yield the formal guarantees linking invariance to bounded performance loss.

sections.heading: VConclusion
sections.text:
We presented a robust MARL framework for sUAS separation assurance under GPS degradation and spoofing. The key contributions are a closed-form adversarial perturbation that bypasses adversarial training while approximating the worst-case perturbation to second order, and a KL-based regularization that provably bounds performance degradation under observation corruption. These are integrated into a PPO algorithm with centralized training but decentralized execution.
The simulation experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the robust MARL policy and its superiority compared to a baseline policy, on maintaining safe separation between the sUASs under GPS degradation and spoofing.

figures.caption: Figure 1: An en-route airspace setting for sUAS package delivery. WP = WayPoint. The UASs flying in this airspace need to maintain safe separation when they are impacted by degraded or spoofed GPS signal. The network includes two routes that first cross at WP 1 (in red), then merge later at WP 7 (light red). Each route spans about 10 10 km, illustrating a compact yet realistic urban scenario for sUAS operations.

Figure 2: Neural network architecture. Ownship and intruder states are independently encoded through fully connected (FC) layers with LeakyReLU activations. Intruder embeddings are aggregated via multi-head attention with pooling. The concatenated representation branches into policy and value heads.

Figure 3: Safety performance under increasing observation corruption. Left : Near mid-air collision (NMAC) count of the small UAS. Right : Minimum separation distance between the aircraft agents achieved per episode. The robust policy maintains near-zero NMACs through R ≈ 0.35 R\approx 0.35 and degrades gracefully beyond, while the nominal policy deteriorates sharply after. Shaded regions indicate ± \pm standard deviation.

Figure 4: Snapshot of high-density sUAS traffic under the robust policy at corruption rate R = 0.35 R=0.35 . Aircraft (triangles) traverse structured routes through waypoints, with separation buffers shown as circles. Yellow indicates aircraft approaching loss of separation; green indicates nominal separation status. Labels display flight ID, speed (knots), and altitude (ft). Despite adversarial GPS perturbations, all aircraft maintain safe separation.

Figure 5: Ablation study isolating the contributions of invariance and anchoring regularization. Left : NMAC count. Right : Minimum separation distance. Invariance regularization without anchoring (blue) destabilizes training, yielding poor performance even at R = 0 R=0 . Anchoring alone (green) preserves nominal behavior but degrades sharply at high R R .
The full method (purple) combines both regularizers for consistent performance. Shaded regions indicate ± \pm standard deviation.
